Question: A male neonate born prematurely at 27 weeks  gestational age was noted to have a erythematous eruption at birth. The neonate was the product of a dichorionic, diamniotic twin pregnancy. The other twin did not have any dermatalogic abnormalities. They were born to a previously healthy woman who had been hospitalized 12 days prior for tocolytic therapy. One day prior to delivery, the mother was noted to have yeast on urinalysis. The presence of epithelial cells in the sample suggested vaginal contamination. Despite tocolytic therapy, labor ultimately progressed the following day and cesarean delivery was performed owing to breech presentation of the unaffected twin. Physical examination of the affected twin revealed an afebrile, 1200 g preterm infant. Skin examination showed 100 to 200 pink papules, 0.05 to 0.2 cm in size, distributed on the trunk, extensor upper arms, and upper legs (Figure, A and B). A subset of the lesions appeared vesiculopustular, but no fluid was evident after attempted unroofing. The diaper area, hands, feet, nails, and oral mucosa were unaffected. The second twin had normal cutaneous examination findings. Laboratory analysis revealed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 6000/ L, which was composed of 21% neutrophils, 38% lymphocytes, 24% monocytes, and 5% eosinophils. Gram stain of lesional scrapings did not reveal bacteria nor WBCs, though potassium hydroxide staining showed numerous yeast forms. Lesional polymerase chain reaction was negative for herpes simplex virus. Bacterial cultures from the skin and the blood were ultimately negative. The placentas from both neonates were sent for pathologic analysis (Figure, C and D).A, Pink papules and vesicopustules were noted on the trunk, arms, and extensor thighs, but spared the diaper area; B, morphology of lesions; C and D, histopathologic images of umbilical cord section. What Is Your Diagnosis?
Options: A:Neonatal candidiasis B:Erythema toxicum neonatorum C:Congenital dermatophytosis D:Congenital cutaneous candidiasis
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
explanation: Gross inspection of the placentas was unremarkable. Microscopic examination of the placenta from the affected twin showed acute subchorionitis, chorioamnionitis, and focal wedge-shaped inflammatory foci at the surface of the umbilical cord (Figure, C). Gomori methenamine silver staining highlighted fungal spores and hyphal elements. These findings are consistent with Candida infection (Figure, D). There was no evidence of fungal infection in the unaffected twin.The patient was treated with intravenous amphotericin B. There was no evidence of extracutaneous dissemination of Candida. The cutaneous eruption resolved within 72 hours.Congenital cutaneous candidiasis (CC) is acquired in utero from direct ascending spread of Candida from the maternal genital tract. The cutaneous lesions of CC are often present at birth and are most commonly distributed on the trunk and extensor extremities, but can be generalized. Sparing of the diaper area and mucosal surfaces is characteristic.1 Nail involvement is variably present.2 Lesions are 0.2- to 0.4-cm pink-red macules, papules, and vesiculopustules.3 Fulminant cases may show bullae, erosions, or even a diffuse burnlike dermatitis.4Congenital cutaneous candidiasis  is rare, with only about 100 cases reported in the literature.3 Risk factors for CC include maternal Candida vulvovaginitis (though most of these cases will not develop CC), the presence of a foreign body in the cervix and/or uterus, and prematurity.3 In this case, maternal Candida vulvovaginitis is suspected to have been present and likely served as a nidus for infection.In patients where CC is suspected, the diagnosis can be established by demonstrating spores and pseudohyphae in skin scrapings and/or by culture of organisms from cutaneous lesions.3 In the correct clinical setting, the diagnosis can also be confirmed by demonstrating the presence of fungal organisms within foci of inflammation in fetal adnexal structures.5 Funisits, inflammation of the umbilical cord, is a hallmark of CC, and can be appreciated microscopically as wedge-shaped inflammatory infiltrates in areas of invasion by Candida organisms.5 In this patient, the diagnosis was established by the presence of numerous yeast in skin scrapings and microscopic involvement of the umbilical cord by fungal organisms (Figure, C and D). Blood, urine, and cerebrospinal fungal cultures, as well as imaging studies, can be used to evaluate for disseminated disease. In this patient, results from such additional workup were negative for disease.Prompt initiation of treatment is critical and is an important factor in determining outcome.6 Prognosis is correlated with birthweight. Neonates weighing  less than 1000 g have a mortality rate of approximately 40%, compared with an 8% mortality rate in those weighing 1000 g or more.3 Treatment is most commonly amphotericin B. Congenital cutaneous candidiasis  is not generally thought to herald underlying immunodeficiency, and workup in this regard is not indicated.A curious observation in this case was that only 1 neonate was affected. Given that separate amniotic sacs and placentas were present in this diamniotic, dichorionic pregnancy, it is possible that direct infectious spread to only one fetus occurred. Along these lines, it has been observed that plaques of Candida in CC are typically found on the inferior pole of the amniotic sac and sometimes directly across from the internal cervical orifice,5 raising the possibility that if 1 fetus was positioned more closely to the internal cervical orifice, selective infection could occur. Indeed, in this case, ultrasonographic examination prior to birth showed that the affected twin was positioned more inferiorly, closest to the cervix, while the unaffected twin was positioned more superiorly in a transverse fashion (becoming breech just prior to cesarean delivery).To our knowledge, this report represents only the second published case of discordant CC in dichorionic, diamniotic twins.7

What Is Your Diagnosis?
